Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty inconsistent. I'd tell myself I'd sit down and focus for a few hours, but then I'd get restless and end up pacing around or doing something with my hands instead. I learn better when I'm actually doing things, not just reading or watching lectures. So I'd often spread materials out, try to make physical notes or diagrams, but the whole sitting-at-a-desk thing got old fast. I'd study in bursts when I could tolerate it.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due. I didn't have a set schedule like 'study every Tuesday at 2pm' or anything like that. When deadlines piled up, I'd cram more hours in, but during quieter weeks I'd drift and not touch course stuff for days. The stress of looming assignments was usually what forced me into action, not any internal discipline on my part.
Q3: Usually a deadline or a notification that something was due soon. I wasn't logging in just to browse or stay ahead. Sometimes I'd get an email reminder and think 'oh right, that exists' and then force myself to check what I was missing. Occasionally I'd log in because I was genuinely curious about something, but that was rare. Mostly it was obligation-driven.
Q4: There were a few weeks mid-semester where I got into a rhythm of checking in every couple of days. I think it happened because assignments were rolling out consistently and I didn't want to fall behind. But that routine didn't stick. Once I hit a boring stretch of content or felt overwhelmed, I'd drop off again. It never became automatic for me.
Q5: The videos were decent when they showed practical examples or demonstrations. I'm kinesthetic, so anything where I could see something being done step-by-step helped me understand better. Discussion posts were occasionally useful when other students asked questions I hadn't thought of. But honestly, most of the resources felt kind of passive and I had to work to make them engaging for myself.
Q6: I ignored a lot of the supplementary readings and optional materials. They felt like extra work without clear payoff, and I was already struggling to stay interested in the required stuff. If something looked like a wall of text with no visual or interactive element, I'd skip it or skim at best. I just couldn't bring myself to care about things that seemed disconnected from what I'd actually be tested on.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it was clearly connected to an assignment or exam, or if it was hands-on in some way. If I could see the direct relevance, I'd push through even when bored. But if something felt like busywork or theoretical fluff, I'd mentally check out. I needed to feel like my effort was going somewhere concrete.
Q8: Optional activities felt like suggestions I could safely ignore. Required ones had weight because they affected my grade. I rarely did optional stuff unless I was procrastinating on something harder and wanted to feel productive without real commitment. There's a different pressure when something is mandatory versus when it's just 'recommended.'
Q9: Yeah, definitely. I'd download lecture slides or PDFs and then study them on my tablet while lying on my bed or walking around. I'd also watch YouTube videos on topics when the course materials felt too dry. Sometimes I'd make physical flashcards or draw diagrams on paper. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the LMS.
Q10: There were plenty of times I was reviewing downloaded notes or re-reading printed materials while the LMS showed nothing. I'd also talk through concepts out loud to myself or explain things to a friend, which obviously doesn't register anywhere. My best studying often happened when I was moving around or doing something tactile, not clicking through a platform.
Q11: All the time. I'd open a video or document once, download it or screenshot the key parts, and then never go back to the LMS for that material. It was easier to have things saved locally where I could access them without logging in. The LMS would show one view, but I might have studied that content multiple times offline.
Q12: Mid-semester was rough. I was juggling multiple courses and just felt burned out and bored with everything. The content wasn't grabbing me, and I started feeling like I was just going through the motions. My attendance dropped during that period, and I'd skip logging in for days. The CBT sessions helped a bit with managing the stress, but the boredom was hard to shake.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was tired or stressed, the LMS felt like a chore I wanted to avoid. If I had other responsibilities piling up, course stuff got pushed aside. Confusion also played a role—if I didn't understand something, I'd sometimes avoid it rather than seek help. My engagement definitely fluctuated based on what else was going on in my life.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd have the LMS open but I was just scrolling mindlessly or clicking through videos without really absorbing anything. My mind would wander, and I'd realize I'd watched ten minutes of a lecture without retaining a single thing. Being present on the platform didn't mean I was actually engaged mentally.
Q15: I postponed assignments pretty often, even when I knew they mattered. Part of it was the boredom—I just couldn't motivate myself to start something that felt tedious. Part of it was anxiety about not doing well, so I'd avoid it until the pressure became unbearable. It's a bad cycle, but I fell into it repeatedly throughout the course.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my main driver. I'd check the LMS more frequently as due dates approached and cram in work at the last minute. Without deadlines, I'd drift. The structure they provided was the only thing keeping me somewhat on track, even if my engagement was rushed and stressful rather than steady.
Q17: Feedback had some effect, but it was mixed. If I got decent feedback, I'd feel briefly motivated. If it was critical or I did poorly, I'd sometimes disengage more because it felt discouraging. I appreciated knowing where I stood, but it didn't fundamentally change my habits. I'd still wait until the next deadline to really engage again.
Q18: The course structure was okay, but it didn't really accommodate my learning style. Everything was set up for reading and watching, not doing. I would've engaged more if there were hands-on projects or interactive elements. The weekly module format gave some rhythm, but it also felt repetitive and easy to tune out.
Q19: The lack of interactive or practical activities made engagement harder for me. I need to move, to build, to apply things physically. Just consuming content passively drained me. On the flip side, clear deadlines and organized modules made it easier to know what was expected, even if I didn't always meet those expectations enthusiastically.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for information, trying to figure out what's going on, or just clicking around without direction. High activity doesn't mean productive activity. You can spend hours on a platform and still be confused or disengaged. Sometimes it's about looking busy without actually accomplishing anything meaningful.
Q21: That resonates with me somewhat. I didn't log in constantly, but I did enough offline work to scrape by with a pass. If someone's efficient with their time or studies in ways that don't show up in logs, they can still perform okay. The LMS doesn't capture everything, so low activity doesn't automatically mean low effort or understanding.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in, what you clicked, and how long you spent on things. But they miss the quality of that time—whether you were actually focused or just zoning out. They also miss offline study, discussions with peers outside the platform, and the emotional state you were in. Numbers don't tell the whole story.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in genuine effort, not just showing up. It's about being mentally present, curious, and willing to push through when things get hard. For me, true engagement is rare—it happens when the material connects to something I find interesting or when I can learn by doing rather than just absorbing.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how bored I was most of the time, or how much I struggled with motivation despite technically completing tasks. It wouldn't capture the offline studying, the physical notes I made, or the times I talked through concepts with myself. It also wouldn't show that the CBT sessions actually helped my mood, even if my engagement patterns didn't change dramatically.
